title: Hoang Nguu Son and the Line I Drew Too Early
summary: A reflective trekking note from Hoang Nguu Son (972m) with more than 150 people from the Zalo team on a 15 km route: early doubt, team pace, ridge views, the summit marker, and the quiet lesson about testing the limits we draw too soon.
author: Nguyen Le Phong
html:
The morning we started up Hoang Nguu Son was not dramatic. It was closer to the kind of morning you almost forget: shoes scraping dirt, a backpack finding its weight on my shoulders, the sun already warm, and a line of pale-blue shirts beginning to move up the slope. I remember feeling less heroic than the photos might suggest. Under the hat and the trekking gloves, there was a very ordinary thought running in my head: maybe this one is a bit beyond me.

I do that more often than I like to admit. Before a project, a role, a difficult conversation, or a mountain, I sometimes draw a line quietly and put myself on the smaller side of it. The line feels practical in the moment. It sounds like self-awareness. Only later do I notice that I had decided the ending before giving the beginning a fair chance.

This was a Zalo team trek, so the climb was never only mine. The trail had the familiar small details of a group trip: matching shirts, bucket hats, numbered backpacks, people checking whether the person behind them was still close. That changed the meaning of the day. Alone, a mountain can become a private argument with yourself. With a team, it becomes a lesson in pace: how to keep moving without turning the climb into a race, and how to pay attention to the people breathing beside you.

The way Znews framed the trip names a detail that is easy to miss when I only look back through my own photos: more than 150 people from Zalo were moving toward the same "roof of Nha Trang." That number does not make the climb more impressive in a loud way. It makes it more human. A large group can still be made of many private conversations with doubt, knees, breath, and the next patch of shade.

The article also makes the route more concrete than memory alone does. The team started preparing before sunrise, the route was stretched to 15 km across both directions, and the climb was organized around five checkpoints before the 972 m marker. Reading those details later, I saw the day less as one spontaneous burst of effort and more as a small system: people walking, people holding stations, water, food, medicine, and enough pauses for a long line to keep moving.

Everyone has a first day

Before the trip, two sentences from my older brother kept coming back. The first was: do not put a ceiling over yourself too early. The second was plainer: everyone has a first day of school. I used to think the first one was the main advice. On the slope, the second one felt more useful. Everyone is awkward on a first day. A first trek. A first demanding project. A first role that feels larger than the confidence we have at the time.

So this is not a story about conquering a mountain. It is a small note about walking past a line I had drawn too quickly, one step at a time, while realizing that other people were walking through their own versions of the same line.

A team is a pace, not a race

In the middle of the climb, speed became much less interesting. Breath mattered more. Water mattered more. Knowing when to pause without treating the pause as failure mattered more. There were stretches where I wanted to push faster, mostly to prove to myself that I was fine. The mountain answered without drama: spend too much energy here, and the steeper part above will collect the debt.

That is also why the support work stayed with me after I read about it. Food, water, medicine, and checkpoint preparation were not background logistics. They were the quiet infrastructure that made courage less lonely. A person can decide to keep going, but a good team makes that decision easier to repeat at the next difficult turn.

A good team pace is not the fastest pace. It is the pace the whole line can keep without leaving anyone behind or pushing anyone into the ground. Someone slows, so you slow. Someone goes quiet, so you offer water before they ask. Endurance, in that setting, is partly a social skill: not being careless with yourself, and not being careless with the people near you. I thought about that while remembering a VnExpress interview with Lê Hồng Minh, where mountains become a way to talk about trying things, testing limits, and lasting. The part that stayed with me was practical, not theatrical: try, learn, keep a safety boundary, sit down when you need to, then continue.

When the mountain opens

There is a moment on a trail when the view suddenly opens and the mind becomes quieter. The water far below turns into a thin bright line. The city shrinks. One ridge leans into another, and the path that felt so large from inside it becomes a smaller line across the mountain. Nothing is solved by that view, but the scale of the question changes. What felt heavy at the start becomes part of a wider landscape.

That is one reason outdoor trips help me return to work better. Work often asks for focus, decisions, deadlines, and a long list of pressures that do not always announce themselves loudly. The trail asks for fewer things, but it asks honestly: breathe, step, watch the ground, protect the place you are passing through, and do not turn every beautiful thing into another task to process quickly. It is not an escape from work. It is a way to come back with a little more room inside.

972 meters

The marker at the top reads Hoang Nguu Son, 972 m, with a faded red star and an old line that says Blue Eye, 2018. It is a small marker on a big rock, but a whole line of tired people had spent the morning walking toward exactly that point.

The marker gives the day a visible point. But if I look only at the marker, I miss the quiet accumulation that made it possible: the preparation, the awkward first steps, the moment I nearly spent too much energy, the decision to rest, the patience to keep a steady pace. A lot of progress in work and life is built the same way. From the outside, it can look sudden. From the inside, it is usually a long line of ordinary choices that no one photographs.

In software, the visible point is usually a release, a migration that lands cleanly, a production fix handled without panic, or a new role. But the real story starts earlier: a clearer pull request, a calmer incident review, a better question in a meeting, a difficult topic learned before it becomes urgent. Progress is rarely loud while it is happening. It sits inside the days when you keep doing one small thing a little better.

What I carried down

Coming down was not a separate reward after the climb. It was part of the same lesson. Many people were descending around noon and early afternoon, when Nha Trang was close to 40 degrees and the body had already spent most of its easy energy. Tired legs make you more honest. Loose conversation makes the morning feel less like a performance and more like something the group actually went through together. The summit gives you a point to remember; the descent gives you time to understand it.

What I carried down from Hoang Nguu Son was not the feeling that I had conquered something. It was the quieter recognition that I had been too quick to make a conclusion about myself. The mountain had not become smaller. My assumption had. And because the climb happened with a team, the lesson did not feel like a slogan about personal willpower. It felt more grounded: prepare what you can, keep a pace you can sustain, rest without shame, and let people help you keep moving.

The line was smaller than I thought

I came back thinking about how easy it is to draw the first line in our heads. Everyone has a first day. Everyone has a first climb, a first project that feels too large, a first role bigger than today's confidence. The point is not to pretend fear is gone. It is to keep enough humility to learn, enough honesty to know where we actually are, and enough courage to test the line instead of only repeating it.

The thought that stayed with me: many of our limits feel solid because we have rehearsed them for a long time. A good journey does not erase them in one dramatic moment. It lets us test them carefully, widen them a little, rest when we need to, lean on the people walking beside us, and keep going with more trust in the quiet accumulation behind us.

If this reminds you of a trail, a project, or a season of learning where the change only made sense later, I would be glad to hear your version. The useful conversations often begin when we compare the ordinary steps, not just the finished photos.
